key_insight: A dislocation-pileup model shows that residual stress in a thin film relaxes nonuniformly and depends strongly on both the initial stress shape and the film geometry.
engineering_relevance: This gives engineers a physics-based way to estimate stress gradients in constrained thin films, which matters for reliability, delamination risk, and residual-stress design.
method: Model initial shear stress relaxation using screw-dislocation pile-ups at the film-substrate interface.
Link plastic strain to dislocation density to derive a fundamental residual-stress equation.
Solve a singular integro-differential stress-profile equation numerically with collocation for multiple initial stress forms.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical solutions for constant, linear, parabolic, and exponential initial stress distributions, showing strong dependence on thickness-to-width ratio and stress profile.
watch_item: The model is analytical-numerical and idealized, so real films may need calibration for material heterogeneity, interface defects, and non-ideal boundary conditions.
paper_title: Residual stress gradient in a thin film within the dislocation pile-up theory
